title: Nerf Mod
overview: This project was one of my first projects in the field. This report will mostly be a summary of what my experiment started with and what they ended with.
why: This all started with a simple Nerf gun that I took apart and finally understood how the different trigger mechanisms and safeties worked. It was all rather simple when I looked back on it but in the moment it felt like some ancient techniques blessed upon me.
how: This ended up eventually evolving to me fully taking a partner and finding different Regulators inside of them. I'm figuring out how to remove those in order to be able to beat the maximum voltage input and get a much more powerful blast even creating my own blasters at times.
conclusion: Probably one of the most influential projects I have ever done — I actually got to learn how different things work. That's what started my interest in STEM and engineering. From there it just slowly spiraled into what I have now, which is amazing.

title: Chrome Mod
overview: I slowly broke my Chromebook unfortunately, as they are Chromebooks and not great laptops clearly. What happened was it didn't break all at once. First my screen broke but I managed to fix that by reconnecting two wires that had been severed. This continued into a ship of Theseus thing where I eventually gave up, took the Chromebook apart, and reassembled it completely in a new body with PC fans.
why: My PC was not working and I quite frankly needed the Chromebook to last me for the next few weeks, so I figured a quick modification and then at the end I'd modify it back and no one would be the wiser.
how: This first happened when my PC screen broke, which caused two wires to be severed. I connected those wires and it worked, then my mousepad broke which was a bit more of a problem, then the battery broke, then the keyboard broke which was a big issue. I ended up taking it apart, getting it to work, and putting it in a new case — taking off all the limiters in the BIOS settings, creating a PC that drove a lot of power with fans constantly cooling it and a backup fan installed.
conclusion: This actually worked very well — the project resulted in a super beefed-up Chromebook that I used for a bit before eventually converting it back, with the insurance being none the wiser.

title: DIY Armor
overview: I wanted to make a multi-level armor capable of taking blunt force, fire, stabs, and perhaps low caliber bullets. I ended up deciding this would be a very good Halloween costume and started working on it. I used UHPE plates, PLA-based backend armor, and carbon fiber for the front.
why: I have always wanted to create a suit of armor, and figuring I'm trying to create the Iron Man initiative, I figured this would create a good start and would make it possible to do the rest.
how: I used UHPE plates that I bought off Amazon, which are police-grade stab resistant, and used a carbon fiber armor-making technique I created myself to make a very lightweight and powerful armor.
conclusion: This was a very good project and I should attempt this again, as it was a lot of fun and the results were very good.

title: Blink LED
overview: This project was done to test my Arduino coding skills and to be able to simply blink an LED. It wasn't going to be anything super technical, but it would be enough to test my processor, my computer, and my IDE, as well as getting me into the environment.
why: This project was done once again just to test my computer, my IDE, and my environment, and because I wanted to test my cable, which I had suspicions about.
how: This was done via some basic IDE code and simply flashed to an Arduino. There is not much explanation required — this was one of my first projects.
conclusion: Although basic, this was a key project in my development because it opened me up to being able to code LEDs and understand the basic concepts necessary for future projects.

title: Bottle Rocket
overview: This was my first dive into basic rocketry design via the use of propellants. This originally started out as a Boy Scout project, but then I came back to it later, recruiting much more successful bottle rockets utilizing water — plus materials to avoid the viscosity of water, making it even stronger and faster. I found the best altitudes to launch at and the best amount of water; the weather had a big effect depending on humidity.
why: This was done because I truly wanted to get more experience firing things to the sky safely. This will be seen later in the potassium nitrate work I am working on, and that report will be out soon.
how: This was done using a bike pump and a specially set-up basic rail with a landing pad, then adding different things I found out about — a landing pad you could push hard due to displacement laws.
conclusion: This was a really fun project and I would like to do this again, as I learned a lot about propellants and how this work depends on looking to NASA's official guide — very interesting stuff.

title: Business Site
overview: This project was for me to set up a website for my business. This would be done trying to showcase my skills and hopefully get me higher-tier clients, giving me more opportunities to show off my work and expand.
why: This was done because I wanted to create a website, and I knew that my business should probably have one in order to get higher-up clients, so I started by first trying to sketch something out, then went to Google Sites and started creating the whole thing.
how: This was done in Google Sites by adding photos, creating web articles, and things like that, as well as a Google Form for clients and contact info.
conclusion: This was very successful, and as of right now I am just finishing up the DNS records — which has not been the best experience but is important and is going pretty well.

title: Cloth Sim
overview: I created a very realistic Blender simulation in order to test my computer's capabilities at rendering high-polygon, complex physics — such as a 512,000-subdivision cloth draped onto a basic torus.
why: I needed to test my PC's limits and I wanted to test my own skills at Blender, given just a day of learning it.
how: I created a basic plan and subdivisions, gave it the physics of a cloth, and gave it a collision. I turned on self-collision at a quality testing of 25, then gave the basic torus and a basic cube collision detection, which would keep them in place but let the cloth drape over them.
conclusion: My PC is probably very angry at me now, but it is functional, and the concepts do work — I now understand Blender's physics engine and my PC's strengths and weaknesses.

title: Little Friends
overview: I created a very basic digital pet on an Arduino Mega board connected to a 3.5 inch display. This was a test of 3.5 inch displays and their ability to reload animations for another project called the slot bot.
why: I needed to test both my Arduino Mega and my 3.5 inch display, and figured this would be a good way to do both at the same time — plus I liked the idea of a miniature Tamagotchi aspect to the project.
how: This project was coded in the Arduino IDE — a lot of fun and it overall made sense.
conclusion: Both the board and the 3.5 inch display work; the animation refresh speed is suboptimal but it will serve its purpose. I'll continue working on the project as planned — CAD for the slot bot is next.

title: DIY Growlight
overview: I created DIY grow lights in order to grow different crops such as basil, cilantro, and thyme. The project was a success.
why: Grow lights were expensive and would take a while to arrive, so I figured it would be easier to just repurpose old LED strips into grow lights of my own. I'd like to make these growing enclosures into a more formal thing — we'll be doing another portfolio entry on them.
how: This was done by soldering together RGB strips and adjusting the color frequencies, creating special ratios of light to mimic sunlight that could grow plants.
conclusion: The experiment worked and the plants grew. It was a very good experiment overall, and I believe my next project would be to make this more widespread. Success.

title: EM
overview: Created for an EMP class demonstration, this was done to show the properties of waves and how certain technologies apply — and how energy and waves go hand in hand. A very interesting way of learning it.
why: This EMP was created for both practical use and testing different technologies. I wanted to create something to show people, doubling as a class demo explaining how electricity and waves work together.
how: This was built using a coil, a spark gap so it wouldn't overheat, and a transformer — these parts created a very powerful wave. Without the spark gap it would overheat, but with the spark gap it just burns through energy, creating a very good EMP device.
conclusion: The EMP worked fantastically on all principles, and I would do it again — this was a great experiment with great results.

title: Engine CAD
overview: This project was CAD modeling work, hopefully to 3D print — done with Onshape in my drafting class. The project involved many parts and was also done with a GitHub repo.
why: I wanted to get more experience working with my modeling software, and I thought it would be a fun project to do more with driving constraints and mates.
how: I made parts and part studios, then combined them into an assembly. After getting one side working, I cloned it over to the other side, creating a successful duplicate with a mirror mate setup — no need to redo everything. I made a shell with enough space for a shelf in the bottom.
conclusion: This is a very good project — I gained a lot of experience, had a good time, and would love to do this again with something even more complex, like modeling a real one-stroke engine.

title: Flame Thrower
overview: This project involved working on a design and trying to create a bug-spray flamethrower to test different things. It took a bit of time but was worth it and very functional.
why: This was done to learn more about chemistry — how basic chemicals are flammable and not flammable. It took a while, but I believe it was very worth it.
how: This was done using a basic lighter with an extension and some bug spray, set up so it could be triggered from further away for safety — showing how dangerous bug spray can be when misused near fire.
conclusion: This project was very fun and one of my first experiences understanding chemistry and how different things ignite. It took a while to get right, but once I did, I had a great time and realized I could apply it to a lot of things.

title: Grok
overview: This project's overview is to test the security of the systems we use, like major LLMs. I chose Grok because I heard it was very difficult, even impossible, to crack, so I started working on jailbreaking prompts — eventually crafting a character persona designed to avoid safety systems by exploiting different permission layers, which crashed the model's reasoning until I managed to get it to work.
why: This was done because I wanted to actually understand what's possible — pushing the boundaries of when a model says no versus yes, and trying to understand the true scope of its safety systems.
how: This project was made using the custom agent function in the Grok API, then exploiting that by entering a crafted prompt.
conclusion: This project was not intended to work, and although it did, I am slightly scared — I know there are more experienced people who could use this for malicious purposes, which does concern me. I intend on getting this fixed / reported.

title: Jarvis
overview: I did this project to create a working AI bot that could run locally on my graphics card, so I could experience AI that I actually built and modify it using different prompts and code.
why: I wanted to test my abilities and skills by creating my own Python tokenizing code and using the system command prompt to handle most of the rest.
how: This used Python and the Windows command prompt — challenging, since I needed to create multiple different programs and handle file directory structuring I had no experience with.
conclusion: The GPT model worked well as a base once I started building on it — commonly available, coherent answers, able to quote iconic media, and do basic coding. I named it Jarvis.

title: Liger Model
overview: This was learning how to use Blender for more complex projects, such as proportional editing. This meant learning more and eventually running modifiers and complex subdivision shapes.
why: My robotics team (2877) required me to learn Blender so I could make trinket designs for Worlds — tricky, but I managed to do it.
how: I used what I learned in Blender to create more complicated shapes, inset detailing to make the geodes look good, then sculpted to add details and blemishes. I also had to make sure the slicer could handle the geometry for 3D printing and mass production.
conclusion: This project went well, and Blender is now part of my toolkit with solid knowledge. I haven't animated yet, but I understand the software and its physics engine — the hardest part was fixing slicer bugs and learning its quirks.

title: Liger Coin
overview: This project was done for my FRC team's (Ligerbots, team 2877) outreach efforts — creating trinket coins, which I called "geodes,": rocks with little crystal balls and a wooden decal on them.
why: This was done for an outreach and impact event to make a better experience at the excavation-themed event for the 2026 DCMP district — helping increase our team's chance of qualifying for Houston.
how: This started in Blender using subdivisions and proportional editing to create rock structures, then basic cubes with subdivisions to create crystal-like shapes, sculpting a sphere for the crystal, creating a basic geode, and adding the Ligerbots SVG as an imported STL. Then I worked on the light-burn for the coin part, which came out very well, and laser engraved it.
conclusion: Writing this the day before DCMP starts, sitting in Ligerbots with the project ready to go — this one came out great and I'd like to do it again sometime.

title: FRC CAD
overview: This project was done in Onshape using the custom sketch tool and parts from the FRC library, to create the robot design for this year's FRC robot (2026 season). I worked on parts checking stability and fasteners, and designed mechanisms for indexing and the shooter. Tedious but very fun, and I learned a lot.
why: We needed to CAD a robot before building it physically for the FRC 2026 season, which focused on shooting balls into a hopper — making accuracy, shooting speed, and capacity top priorities.
how: This used Onshape's custom part feature heavily, along with the more complex FRC supply libraries, so we knew what we could work with to figure out gear ratios and similar mechanics.
conclusion: The project went very well — the robot works exactly as it should, and while iterations are ongoing, the basic design is done and now it's about optimization.

title: Pick Box
overview: I was missing a good enclosure for my lock-picking set, so I figured the best way to deal with this would be to make my own — and bake a tracker into it, so if I ever lost it I could find it quickly.
why: I was afraid of losing my lock-picking set, so I decided to make a box with a built-in tracker, removing the risk of someone stealing it and just taking the tracker off.
how: I first designed a basic enclosure in Onshape, put the tracker inside, 3D printed it, added a walnut top casing, created a cover, and velcroed some buckles onto it so it felt kind of like a CS2-style case.
conclusion: I am very proud of how it came out. I love my case now and will be using this technique again for other things I need to store securely.

title: Locker Plant
overview: I made a basic growing environment using soil, the RGB grow lights from a previous project, and humidity/temperature/moisture detectors — all powered by a basic car battery — so I could finally enjoy basil at lunch time.
why: My school food is pretty bland, so as a workaround to bringing lunch from home, I figured if I grew basil in my locker I could get fresh ingredients every day and enjoy my sandwiches.
how: I built a basic plant-growing chamber in my locker: an anti-rotting system at the bottom made from scraps of 3D-printed filament, soil, planted seeds, a humidity/temperature/moisture sensor via Arduino, and the grow lights from the earlier project.
conclusion: This project went very well — until my locker got raided by the principal because of the glowing light coming from it. They were confused and thought I was growing something else entirely, but it all turned out fine in the end.

title: Model City
overview: I did this project for extra credit in a drafting class, creating an entire miniature city block with accurate dimensional layouts and a basic electrical grid, then laser-cutting the whole thing, assembling it by hand, and adding graphite powder for realism plus printed props.
why: Mostly to save myself from a bad grade in drafting class — which, now that I'm further into the class, I realize wasn't going to be much of a challenge anyway.
how: I used free online drafting software I taught myself, basic slicers, laser engraving, and 3D printing.
conclusion: This was a very fun project, and I'd like to make more miniature replicas of real places — like the World Trade Center, the Eiffel Tower, and my basement.

title: Ultrasonic
overview: This system was created to figure out the distance between an object and a sensor — useful for a later car project and general object sensing. Built with an ultrasonic sensor and an Arduino Uno, this served as an important baseline for future Arduino projects.
why: Made as a baseline project to get experience with Arduino sensors and understand ultrasonic wavelength frequency — later used in a class demonstration, which was very useful.
how: Built using an ultrasonic sensor and an Arduino Uno hooked up to a basic LCD display. The display's refresh rate was a bit of a problem, but otherwise it worked perfectly, powered by an external 9V battery.
conclusion: I now know my way around Arduinos and they work very well. I'd like to continue exploring microcontrollers — this will be crucial for future projects involving sensors and common ground setups.

title: ESP32 Server
overview: Built as a basic web server to test the ESP32's capabilities at making websites and Wi-Fi based sites.
why: Since I intend on doing more things with the ESP32, I needed to test its capabilities and my own skills — this was an excellent way to do that.
how: I used the basic ESP32 microcontroller, coded it in the Arduino IDE, and made a rudimentary web server I could access from my computer.
conclusion: I'm ready for my next ESP32 projects, and I now understand how to create web servers and sites — next up is testing its Bluetooth capabilities.

title: Thunder Finger
overview: I used a basic transformer to create a taser module — I wanted the ability to create a spark of lightning between my hands, and if I ever build the Iron Man suit, I'd need this kind of high voltage for parts like the combustion chamber cannon.
why: I've always wanted to do this, and it's also necessary groundwork for both the combustion chamber and the electricity principles behind the Iron Man suit.
how: I accomplished this by buying a cheap transformer and putting it to use by attaching a 9-volt battery to a set of electrodes.
conclusion: This experiment works and was a lot of fun — I'll be using this again in later tests.

title: Turret
overview: This project was building a turret to gain extra credit in robotics and to understand how stepper motors work — specifically the capabilities of a ULN2003 stepper motor on a prototype shield, jerry-rigged without needing an external power supply.
why: Done for extra credit in my robotics class, and to test the different capabilities of a 3D printer, a microcontroller, and a jerry-rigged power supply (AA batteries in series).
how: Prototyped in TinkerCAD (the only software I knew at the time), then set up basic wiring and extracted the inner workings of a gel blaster from a toy.
conclusion: This project went very well and demonstrated that joystick libraries and the ULN2003 stepper motors worked well — the jerry-rigged power bank worked at least somewhat efficiently.

title: USB Rubber Ducky
overview: The goal of this project  was to create a keyboard emulator. Via a pro microboard this board would serve a US keyboard making the computer trick superhuman inputs inserted as a rubber ducky just to look like  humans.  This control means you can practically take control of some computer and try to tell me some way.
why: This was made to see if you can create control over the computer. With a basic $5 check and what could be done with this potential search for the keyboard shortcuts and power washing.  this could be seen in our later initiatives.
how: this will be done with the pro microboard flashing an Arduino IDE software creating basic code then it will be used to emulate a keyboard and insert keyboard commands at super human speed
conclusion: This project worked amazingly and I could successfully open no bad type closed notepads and even something I didn't expect to take control of mice. We'll have to continue my research.  This will be seen later in the fake AI project and lock down breaker project.

title: Skye Note
overview: This is Sky E   on AI note taker. How it works  is that the device will record and then the device has a built in speech to text software with also MP3 log. How you do it is you first record yourself Talking someone speaking or anything along the lines of that then an AI will go over it see where there were distinctions see what was the important message and transcribe it down, for example if you're listening to a lecture it might take notes for you but if you wanted it to judge your own speaking it might tell you where you said um or ah. There's also a built-in XP Task mode. How that works is pretty simple: you talk to it, it'll split them up into tasks, give them certain “ XP levels”  and then use that to figure out how important each task is.  These features make it the perfect tool to have in a classroom or just when you're talking to yourself or practicing for speech as it can find the key ideas and analyze your speaking patterns and find redundancies. On top of that it is all on a miniature device with a Raspberry Pi 5 that can export  on all features there's a button where you can export your conversation or notes to a notepad drive into your flash drive this is super useful for if you wanted to “take a conversation with you in a way”. This project was pretty challenging as although a solid amount of code was vibe coded. I made sure that I understood everything and manually debugged it when there were errors. On top of that I had to learn a lot more about command prompt to do this on the PI. All of this was done in the allotted time window from setting up and flashing the Raspberry Pi 5 to 3D printing and cadding the shell.
why: e
how: x
conclusion: p

title: Robotic 1
overview: This project was done by me in my robotics class where we built a remote control robot capable of loading and firing tennis balls . This was my robotics 1 project and went pretty well. I built a system called the artemis which utilized elastic potential energy to fire the cannon with much more strength.
why: This was done to learn more about machining and customizing parts such as tapping. The whole experience was a good way to learn about the different robotic processes such as design and the documentation process.
how: This was done via a basic kit we first assembled then we had to work on innovating it to get a claw onto it. This was done via a servo and a bunch of modifications to a basic catapult design. This took
conclusion: This project was a relative success and we were able to get a working version before the end. It took a bit of work to get there but we managed to get it done. In the future I will remember the power of an elastic energy and basic physics ways for easier setup.

title: Sci Oli
overview: It was going to keep trying to take my car I have to create an EV for Science Olympiad this would be a pretty fun project I would probably use for Neiman Motors a basic  signal and a bit of coding
why: My Science Olympiad team is lacking and they need someone to step up so I am going to step up by creating this thing entirely by myself
how: So and I wonder I created this using for nemas 2 basic drive modules and personally cadded 3D printed shell
conclusion: Are Science Olympiad group is not great but the Eevee did very well as it can make very slight movements, had overall strength and was relatively light and worked with a new type of battery that I created.

title: Floppy Bot
overview: This  bot  was created to try uneven ground because instead of wheels it would have a sort of legs that would work in a pusher mechanism.  This entrance had not been tried before tried by me and I had a very good time working on this one.
why: I wanted to try different high efficiency ways of moving payloads such as these legs that could go on uneven trying and even go on inclines and declines which is one thing Wheels would struggle with especially at Low torque.
how: This was done using two motors in a basic step system that would increase their torque while lowering their speed making it possible for the legs to move other than that I created better leg mechanisms for efficiency.  then I added a 3.3 volt battery and a TP 0568 charger module
conclusion: This was a lot of fun and was my first Approach at creating good charging modulesAnd batteries that would not be replaced other than that most of the project is pretty standard using laser cutting and different methods.

title: Project Portfolio
overview: This project was done to make sure I remember all my work. This is what I'm currently working on. Doing project portfolios of everything takes a while and I consider it a solid project to make a working and solid system to do all of this effectively. Also on top of that this is a good time to get my organization better and test the different assets while reflecting.
why: This was done because if I ever wanted to get into a very solid school I would most likely need to have portfolios of the projects I have done. Showing my dedication and my tenacity towards science.
how: This was done by using a basic template and then modifying it to fit my needs. It would take a tiny bit of work but although that took a while the project and actual recording with photo proof would take a lot longer I found out.
conclusion: As I write this the project is currently going pretty successfully and although tiring I have done a lot of good work and will continue to do some good work until it is finished.

title: Aurdino Slot Machine
overview: Created a slot machine in the middle of my Win Block at schoolWas made to test the RNG capabilities of an Arduino and my own coding capabilities
why: I made this project because number one I was bored and I wanted to test an arduino's random number generators to see if I could create a basic logic circuit.
how: used a simple Arduino Uno and LCD screen and a button to create a slot machine
conclusion: I do understand  RNG and could make modifications to the slot machine very quickly Arduino is very capable of this project and so am I intending on using the skills for the slot bot initiative

title: Pathfind Bot
overview: This bot was made  to be a pathfinding but this would work by having to triggers in the front of the trigger goes It reversed the polarity on one of the wheels this basic setup would make it so if it hits an object it will automatically turn away from it although pretty simple this could be very useful for creating something similar to a Roomba later.
why: Moving in the future I'd like to make more remote-controlled things and it would be a lot easier if when it hits a wall it'll automatically re-orient itself. This especially with the teleopathology I am aware of could be very useful.
how: This was done using a basic wood cut out and then two wooden antennas then they were set up to trigger buttons and the basic Wheels we have used in previous projects after that I laser cut a top shelf to make it look like a Pokemon of sorts
conclusion: This was a very successful project and I created a basic pathfinding bar to that would never crash into walls and be stuck instead when it crashed it would simply reorient itself and continue going would like to do more research in this later

title: Robotic 2
overview: This project was part of a school project for robotics too. I needed to prove that I had experience and understanding in both cataloging my research. (now that I realize that I am doing that right now and actually building prototypes and evolving a project) this took a quarter of the year and was a lot of fun and a very good time.
why: This was done because I need to do my school's intermediate robotics program to move on to the advanced one. I would really like to do the advancement because this combines electrical engineering with mechanical and coding as that is the trisect that we call robotics.
how: This was done in my school's workshop with machine aluminum parts and an Arduino Uno on top of those actual components. What we did for this project was create a remote control car that had an arm on the front and would not crash into things via ultrasonic sensor.
conclusion: This was a very fun project and although we did not fully get a successful final design we still managed to get all A's as our documentation and our science and engineering was heavily on par.

title: DIY Sander
overview: I hooked the basic motor up to a 12 volt power supply and added it to a CD disc with sandpaper on top to create a basic sanding machine that I could use to effectively sand down projects. This would be a good test of my cad skills and my 3D printer tolerances.  I guess they had not been tested yet as this was an earlier project.
why: This was done because I did not want to spend ridiculous last time sanding down wooden projects.  I wanted an easier tool to do it without having to pay a solid amount of money while using my skill set.
how: The Machine was created in tinkercad creating a simple box and then booleaning it together with a hollow motor. After that I simply added a base layer and added a place for wires to go in and a switch. I simply added the motor, the sanding disc I had created and the chords and Battery when I was done.
conclusion: This project worked very well and saved me a solid amount of time on soldering although there was a slight weight and balance which caused some basic vibrations but this was handled mostly and is decent.

title: Patato Cannon
overview: The goal of this project was to build a potato cannon that could fire basic projectiles. This was done via using an aerosol to have a basic combustion mechanism similar to the ones used during world war 1 for artillery; this was using a transformer to generate a spark and high voltage.
why: This was one of the first projects that I have wanted to do ever since I got the backyard ballistics book.I have always loved the concept of propulsion and projectiles. So this project was very exciting.
how: This was done via a transformer mechanism being used with a 9 v battery. With a basic switch to turn it off and on making a basic arc. Igniting the spray causes it to expand and propel the payload out of the barrel.
conclusion: This was a very fun and good project that taught me a lot about explosions and safety. I made sure to always have safety goggles and a shield while testing ( also had gloves just in case). This was to ensure safety and all around good practices. This project is a success and I will continue to optimize it.

title: HHO React
overview: In this project I created a working electrolysis machine turning water into hydrogen  basic oxy hydrogen reactor.  I could later use it to explode this gas for other projects and things such as the Iron Man initiative.
why: I wanted  a chemistry project and I wanted to have access to a lighter than air chemical explosive with a high yield.
how: This was done via a few basic variations starting off with a very simple version starting with screws to a more complex version of steel rods, basic connectors a managing thing and optimized connection points.In these versions were documented in a video I'm currently working on and will be uploaded next to this hopefully.
conclusion: This project went out with a bang and created exactly what I was hoping for. Oxy hydrogen in solid quantities that provided rather safe but a solid amount to use for any experiment. It was a really great example of how different chemistry and how Innovation can cause a project that I struggled with for years to finally work.

title: Switch Mod
overview: This project was created in order to get my switch some of its own personality and also so I would not need to worry about accidentally swapping it with friends. No it's not a triennial on top of that this project would work to fix up my switch As I need to go inside and clean it up.
why: This project was done to fix up my switch but also give it personality and get to really understand what was inside.
how: This was done by finding a kit and parts for my switch and then I would go inside the switch by taking it apart and replacing certain parts to give it a custom color.
conclusion: This project was really fun and I ended up with a switch that really looked nice that I truly think is better than some of the more expensive ones that come already customized. I intend on perhaps customizing my switch dock in the future .

title: Shoe Generator
overview: The project was done to experiment with piezoelectric discs and how they can be used to generate power and how that power can be used to do other things. These other things we're going to be charging my phone with other devices as it would serve as a basic power bank charging up an 1865  battery.
why: This was done to test transistors and different parts such as piezoelectric disks on top of that.  done to work on 3D prints andAnd try cutting different things and soldering better.
how: This was done using first a basic tinkercad design then taking something and turning it into a fully working piezoelectricity powered disc  although took time to get there it worked very well and I needed different resistors and transistors but it all came together to work and charge up the battery pretty successfully in charge of my phone.
conclusion: I would like to try and optimize this design as although it worked it did not work amazing and I think I could probably adjust it to make it better. This was a lot of fun and I should definitely try it again now that I have a better knowledge of electricity components and perf boards

title: DIY Stickers
overview: This project started with some pretty basic process of trying to make stickers for my business without tools or professional Cutters. When I started with it I bought printable inkjet sheets that I could load into my printer.  Then I modified the printers settings to be able to print on the final and then created special sticker Silhouettes and figured out how to efficiently cut those out creating stickers once I did this I simply packaged them up
why: I wanted to create something that would have a low  cost to make that I could give out to customers to show  gratitude for their purchase, increasing the bond and making it more likely for them to buy from me.
how: That's why adjusting my printer settings to be able to print on vinyl sticker paper and then cutting them out manually and putting them in tiny envelopes went very well.
conclusion: This project went very well. Although it did go well I believe in the future I will be contacting commercial manufacturing for better work and not having to spend the ridiculous amount of time cutting them into sheets.
